title: Emergency Response

jokes.text: "Dynamite" stands for daring, youth-friendly, natural, amazing, merry, inviting, tourist attraction-y, and eco-friendly.
jokes.setup: Leslie says she can sum up Pawnee Commons in one word: Dynamite
jokes.punchline: She then creates an elaborate, forced acronym with "tourist attraction-y" as a highlight
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie over-preparation and awkward acronym creation. The made-up word 'tourist attraction-y' is perfectly Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can sum up Pawnee Commons in one word: Dynamite.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: D-Y-N-A-M-I-T-E. Dynamic. Young. Nostalgic. Aspirational. Modern. Innovative. Tourist attraction-y. Exciting.

jokes.text: Let the record show there was a standing ovation. / No, there wasn't. / There was not. / History will decide.
jokes.setup: Jamm claims there was a standing ovation for his presentation
jokes.punchline: Multiple people immediately contradict him, but he doubles down with 'History will decide'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Jamm's delusional confidence, though not groundbreaking

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: There was a standing ovation for my presentation.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: No there wasn't.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Everyone was sitting down the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: History will decide.

jokes.text: We make money. They make money. They make burgers. We eat burgers. That's a win-win-win-win.
jokes.setup: Jamm explains his simple plan for the lot
jokes.punchline: His logic chain is so basic it becomes absurd, especially the quadruple-win conclusion
jokes.explanation: Solid Jamm character comedy with his oversimplified business logic

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Okay, here's my plan for the lot. We sell it to a developer. Developer builds luxury condos. People buy the condos. They live in the condos. It's a win for the developer, a win for the people living in the condos, a win for the city because of increased tax revenue, and a win for me because I facilitated the deal.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: Quadruple-win.

jokes.text: Presentation over. Jamm out.
jokes.setup: Jamm finishes his extremely brief presentation
jokes.punchline: His casual 'mic drop' style exit after such a minimal effort
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment, fits Jamm's lazy confidence

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: That's all I got.

dialogue.speaker: Jeremy Jamm
dialogue.line: *drops mic*

jokes.text: Paunch Burger's number-one-selling burger is the greasy lard bomb.
jokes.setup: Leslie claims she's not editorializing about greasy lard bombs
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's literally the name of their bestselling burger
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of expectations - what sounds like insult is actually the product name

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm not editorializing. That's literally what they call it. The Greasy Lard Bomb is their bestselling burger.

jokes.text: Love fades away. But things... Things are forever.
jokes.setup: Wedding registry employee gives relationship advice
jokes.punchline: Advises that material possessions outlast love in a disturbingly matter-of-fact way
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny wisdom from a retail worker, unexpectedly profound and cynical

dialogue.speaker: Store Employee
dialogue.line: You know what? Material possessions are forever. Love? Love fades. But this KitchenAid mixer? This will outlast your marriage. Guaranteed.

dialogue.speaker: Store Employee
dialogue.line: I've seen it a thousand times. Couples come in here all starry-eyed, pick out their china patterns, and then bam—six months later, he's gone. But that Cuisinart is still running strong.

jokes.text: That's beautiful. Did the Dalai Lama say that?
jokes.setup: The employee just gave cynical advice about love vs. material goods
jokes.punchline: Ben genuinely asks if this materialistic wisdom came from the Dalai Lama
jokes.explanation: Good ironic misattribution, Ben missing the cynicism completely

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Did the Dalai Lama tell you that?

jokes.text: Ooh! A citrus reamer! Oh, this is the only way to ream citrus. You're gonna need two of these.
jokes.setup: Leslie gets excited about wedding registry items
jokes.punchline: Her intense enthusiasm for an obscure kitchen gadget and insistence they need two
jokes.explanation: Typical Leslie over-enthusiasm for mundane domestic items

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at this! A serrated bread knife with an ergonomic grip. We NEED this.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, we already have three bread knives.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Not like this one, we don't. And actually, we need TWO of these. One for bread, one for... other breads.

jokes.text: people could bid to put their names on everything in the park, like benches, cobblestones, trees, ants, air
jokes.setup: Ben suggests naming rights for park features
jokes.punchline: List escalates from reasonable (benches) to impossible (ants, air)
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke with increasingly absurd naming rights

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: We could sell naming rights to park features. You know, 'The Sweetums Bench,' 'The Paunch Burger Gazebo.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's actually a great idea, Ben.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: We could sell naming rights to anything. 'The JJ's Diner Drinking Fountain,' 'The Kwame Jackson Trashcan.'

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ooh, what about 'The Rent-A-Swag Tree'?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Sure. 'The Sweetums Ant Colony.' 'The Paunch Burger Air.'

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're not naming the air, Ben.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude.

jokes.text: I'm really feeling these sheets--very cozy.
jokes.setup: Ben and Leslie just had their romantic breakthrough moment
jokes.punchline: Cut to Ron completely ignoring the moment, focused on bedding quality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - oblivious to romance, focused on practical comfort

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: These are Egyptian cotton sheets. 1,200 thread count. I had them specially ordered from a supplier in Cairo.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't believe in romance, but I do believe in high-quality linens.

jokes.text: Well, if I told you to jump off a bridge, would you do it?
jokes.setup: Leslie tells team to applaud, then criticizes them for premature applause
jokes.punchline: She uses the classic parent phrase after literally telling them to applaud
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment showing Leslie's contradiction and control issues

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, applaud.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, no, no, no. Why are you applauding? I didn't say anything yet.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is exactly like when my kids clap before I finish the sentence. I hate that.

jokes.text: I'm omelet. Get it? "I'm on it," "I'm omelet"? / I get it, and I love it, but I don't have time for food puns right now. / Okay, I'm heading out. Good-pie. / Go. / Gurt. Go-gurt. I'm incredible.
jokes.setup: Tom makes a food pun when Leslie asks him to handle vendors
jokes.punchline: Despite Leslie shutting him down, he continues with increasingly forced food puns
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom character moment - can't resist wordplay even when told to stop

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, can you handle the vendors?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Sure, I'll make sure everything is on a roll.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Please don't.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'll ketchup with them later.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, stop.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This is going to be egg-cellent. I mean, lettuce be honest, I'm on a roll. Lettuce, get it?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm walking away now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, you're not even going to say I'm the cream of the crop? Because I'm dairy good at this.

jokes.text: Good-pie.
jokes.setup: Tom leaving after being told no food puns
jokes.punchline: Immediately does another food pun
jokes.explanation: Tom can't help himself - does exactly what he was told not to do. The defiance is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Fine, fine. I'll stop with the food puns. I guess you could say I'm going cold turkey.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Gayle. My girls!
jokes.setup: Emergency drill is announced
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate panic about his family during a fake emergency
jokes.explanation: Typical Jerry overreaction, fits character but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh no, oh no, oh no! My family! I have to call my family!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Jerry, it's just a drill.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A drill?! Well why didn't you say so! Phew!

jokes.text: Oh, this is just a drill, but I am having so much fun pretending it's real.
jokes.setup: Ron explains it's just a drill after Jerry's panic
jokes.punchline: His deadpan admission that he enjoys the fake chaos
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron deadpan - finds enjoyment in others' panic with characteristic honesty

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's just a drill.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have to admit, I do enjoy the fake chaos.

jokes.text: Every time it so much as drizzles in Pawnee, the town is in danger of collapsing into Thunderdome-style, post-apocalyptic mayhem.
jokes.setup: Leslie reads from Pawnee's emergency preparedness report
jokes.punchline: Official report compares light rain to Mad Max-level societal collapse
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from drizzle to Thunderdome, great absurdist government incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: According to Pawnee's emergency preparedness report, a light drizzle is comparable to a Mad Max-level societal collapse.

jokes.text: Should someone stop Jerry? / Gayle! / Eh, he'll figure it out eventually.
jokes.setup: Jerry is presumably still running around panicking about the fake emergency
jokes.punchline: The team casually discusses whether to help him while he continues yelling for his wife
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy implied - Jerry lost in panic while others are blasé

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Help! Someone help me! Margaret! Margaret!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Should we do something?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I mean, he seems to be handling it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm having a heart attack! Margaret!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: He's definitely panicking.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yeah, but is it our problem?

jokes.text: people always say, "But he's got a great personality."
jokes.setup: Andy feels confident about the personality evaluation
jokes.punchline: His evidence is the backhanded compliment typically given to unattractive people
jokes.explanation: Good ironic self-awareness failure - Andy misses the insult completely

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm not worried about the personality evaluation. I'm very confident in my personality.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: People are always telling me, 'You have such a great personality.'

jokes.text: I picture 35 a-salt shakers and 42 batteries.
jokes.setup: Andy explains his mnemonic device for assault and battery
jokes.punchline: He literally pictures 'a-salt' shakers for 'assault'
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Andy - takes wordplay too literally in his learning method

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So for assault, I picture a giant salt shaker. A-salt. Get it?

jokes.text: I picture 533 salt shakers and batteries lighting a building on fire.
jokes.setup: Andy continues explaining his mnemonic system
jokes.punchline: Now hundreds of salt shakers are committing arson in his mental image
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the salt shaker concept into increasingly absurd scenarios

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Now hundreds of salt shakers are committing arson in his mental image.

jokes.text: And if you don't, I'll just divorce you and marry someone else and then cheat on them with you.
jokes.setup: April tries to be supportive about Andy's police test
jokes.punchline: Her 'encouragement' involves an elaborate infidelity scheme
jokes.explanation: Perfect April - twisted logic that's somehow romantic in her own way

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're going to do great on that test. And if you don't pass, I have a backup plan. I've already contacted three women from your high school and I'm working on a fourth. If you fail the police exam, you can move in with one of them, start a new family, and leave all this behind.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm being supportive. That's what you wanted, right?

jokes.text: I found a dead rabbit on the side of the road, and I cut its feet off and made it into a lucky charm.
jokes.setup: April gives Andy a 'lucky charm' for his test
jokes.punchline: She made it by mutilating roadkill
jokes.explanation: Classic April macabre gesture presented as sweet romantic gift

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: She made it by mutilating roadkill

jokes.text: Baby, you are so creepy. Thank you. I love it.
jokes.setup: April just gave him lucky rabbit feet from roadkill
jokes.punchline: Andy finds her creepiness endearing and is genuinely grateful
jokes.explanation: Sweet Andy/April relationship dynamic - he loves her darkness

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, this is the sweetest, creepiest thing anyone has ever done for me. Thank you.

jokes.text: My mother is getting a colonoscopy today. I'm not even sure there's anyone there to drive her home, so we're all making sacrifices, Ms. Knope. / Well, I'm very sorry to hear about your mother. / Mm, we're not that close. / Oh. Good.
jokes.setup: Leonard explains his personal sacrifice to be there, Leslie offers sympathy
jokes.punchline: He reveals he doesn't actually care about his mother, Leslie awkwardly adjusts
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke with Leonard's unexpected coldness catching Leslie off-guard

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: I had to leave my mother's deathbed to be here.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I'm so sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Don't be. She was a real bitch.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Went past the zero. I got to wait till it goes... Okay, now.
jokes.setup: Leonard is about to start the drill with dramatic timing
jokes.punchline: He's thrown off by his stopwatch and has to restart his dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Minor visual comedy about bureaucratic precision ruining dramatic timing

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Alright everyone, gather around. I'm about to say something very important.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Wait, my stopwatch... hold on.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Okay, starting over. Alright everyone, gather around. I'm about to say something very important.

jokes.text: Nope, can't fly away. This is real life.
jokes.setup: Leslie thinks she might be in a nightmare
jokes.punchline: She tests for dream logic by trying to fly, confirming reality
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment - Leslie's logical approach to testing if she's dreaming

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, if this is a nightmare, I should be able to fly.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna jump off this desk and fly.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Nope. Still on the ground. This is real.

jokes.text: Yes! Avian flu! Jackpot. This is a simple one, guys.
jokes.setup: Leonard announces they've been hit by avian flu
jokes.punchline: Leslie celebrates a deadly pandemic as 'jackpot' because it's easy to handle
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie moment - her over-preparation makes disasters seem manageable

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: We've been hit by avian flu.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jackpot! Do you know how easy avian flu is to handle? It's just birds. We don't even like birds. They're basically dinosaurs that never evolved.

jokes.text: "Mission Im-Pawnee-able: Knope Protocol."
jokes.setup: Leslie corrects someone about her emergency protocol name
jokes.punchline: The elaborate pun title she created for her disaster plan
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie over-clever naming with multiple puns crammed together

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's not an emergency protocol, it's a disaster preparedness initiative. And it's called 'The Lesslie Knope Guide to Knope-ing with Disaster.' Get it? Knope? Like my last name? Because I'm hoping we can knope our way through any crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've been working on that pun for three years.

jokes.text: Hello, my name is Donatella Breckinridge, M.D. I graduated first in my class from Harvard Medical School, so I know what I'm talking about. This is the avian flu, or we call "H5N1."
jokes.setup: Donna appears on the emergency drill video as a medical expert
jokes.punchline: She adopts an elaborate fake identity with over-the-top credentials
jokes.explanation: Good Donna moment - she commits fully to playing an expert with fake prestige

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Dr. Donna Meagle, MD, PhD, DDS, and several other letters.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I've been practicing emergency medicine for over forty years, and I've saved thousands of lives.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I'm also a world-renowned surgeon, an acclaimed chef, and I speak seventeen languages fluently.

jokes.text: Great leadership-- inspiring.
jokes.setup: Donna just gave minimal delegation instructions
jokes.punchline: Ron's deadpan sarcastic assessment of her 'leadership'
jokes.explanation: Typical Ron dry commentary, well-delivered but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That was the most inspiring delegation I've ever heard. I'm going to go start a small business based solely on that speech.

jokes.text: Fair point. I did cry last time.
jokes.setup: Ron points out he won't cry on TV unlike Ben
jokes.punchline: Ben readily admits to crying on television
jokes.explanation: Good Ben character moment - his emotional vulnerability acknowledged matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I would never cry on television.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I have. Multiple times. It's not something I'm proud of, but it happened.

jokes.text: I'm from Orlando. And I don't care about the Paunch Burger. If it was another French restaurant, maybe I'd worry.
jokes.setup: Tom appeals to the chef's 'refined Parisian sensibilities'
jokes.punchline: Chef reveals he's from Orlando and only cares about French competition
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - punctures Tom's assumptions about French chef sophistication

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm appealing to your refined Parisian sensibilities.

dialogue.speaker: Chef
dialogue.line: I'm from Orlando. I only care about French competition cooking shows.

jokes.text: What you got for me? Get out of my kitchen! / Okay, okay, okay. I'm sorry, sorry, sorry. Pardon! Pardon!
jokes.setup: Chef gets increasingly angry at Tom's continued presence
jokes.punchline: Tom desperately switching between English apologies and French 'pardon'
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy sequence with Tom's panic-driven language switching

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I'm sorry, pardon, pardon!

dialogue.speaker: Chef
dialogue.line: Get out!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Pardon! Je suis désolé! I apologize!

dialogue.speaker: Chef
dialogue.line: OUT!

jokes.text: Pardon! Pardon!
jokes.setup: Tom being thrown out by angry Orlando chef
jokes.punchline: Still trying to use French to seem sophisticated while fleeing
jokes.explanation: Tom desperately clinging to his French affectation even while being ejected. Shows his commitment to the bit.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Au revoir, chef! C'est la vie!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Bon appétit! Je m'en vais!

jokes.text: Right. So how do I kill 'em-- like, with a gun? / No. / I could fill up a bathtub and just drown 'em one at a time.
jokes.setup: Brett asks for clarification on the pretend bird killing
jokes.punchline: He escalates to increasingly elaborate and disturbing methods
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation comedy - Brett's enthusiasm for fake bird murder gets uncomfortable

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying you want me to pretend to kill birds? Like, what does that even mean?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know, just act it out. Pretend you're hunting them.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Okay, so I could use a gun? Or maybe set traps? What about poison? I could poison them. Or use my bare hands and strangle them one by one. I could build a giant bird cage and slowly starve them. I could—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, okay! Just... just pretend to shoo them away or something.

jokes.text: Kill all birds. This is for the drill, right? Yes. But I'm actually gonna kill these birds for real? No. Just pretend.
jokes.setup: Leslie giving animal control instructions during drill
jokes.punchline: Brett's confusion about actually killing vs. pretending to kill birds
jokes.explanation: Brett's literal interpretation of drill instructions creates absurd moral confusion about simulated vs. real bird murder.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay everyone, this is a drill. We're pretending to kill the birds.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Pretending?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes, Brett. It's a drill.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Oh, because I've been actually killing them.

jokes.text: Okay, let's forget we ever talked. / Got it. Kill 'em.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to abort the conversation about bird killing
jokes.punchline: Brett interprets this as confirmation to proceed with the killing
jokes.explanation: Good miscommunication joke - Brett's selective hearing for violent instructions

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Let's not talk about this anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: Okay, so we're doing this. I'll get the shotgun.

jokes.text: Not so fast. I regret to inform you that someone in this room has begun exhibiting symptoms-- Christopher Traeger.
jokes.setup: Leslie thinks they're doing well with low casualties
jokes.punchline: Leonard reveals Chris is now infected, using his full formal name
jokes.explanation: Decent setup/punchline structure with formal name reveal adding drama

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're doing well with low casualties.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Actually, Chris Pratt is now infected.

jokes.text: A few months ago, the thought of an infectious disease, even hypothetical, would have sent me careening towards Bummerville, but now I am infected with a killer virus, and I feel fine. Therapy!
jokes.setup: Chris should be panicking about being infected in the drill
jokes.punchline: Instead he celebrates this as proof of his therapeutic progress
jokes.explanation: Excellent Chris character moment - turns fake death into personal growth victory

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Wait, I've been exposed to a deadly virus and I'm not panicking? This is huge! Do you know what this means? I'm finally emotionally stable!

jokes.text: That is impossible. I do not ride the bus. I ride my bicycle behind the bus as a windbreak.
jokes.setup: Chris is accused of riding the bus while infected
jokes.punchline: He reveals his bizarre transportation method of cycling behind buses
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist character moment - perfectly bizarre Chris fitness logic

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I wasn't on the bus. I was cycling behind it the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You were... cycling behind the bus?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: It's called drafting. It's very efficient. The bus breaks the wind resistance, and I just follow along at a safe distance.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Chris, that might be the weirdest thing you've ever done, and you once ate a whole rotisserie chicken in the park at 6 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: That was different. That was about protein optimization.

jokes.text: I should've had animal control kill you. / Oh, who you want me to kill? / No one. / I'll kill him... As soon as I'm done with these birds.
jokes.setup: Leslie jokingly says she should have had Brett killed
jokes.punchline: Brett eagerly offers murder services and promises to get to it after the birds
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Brett's murder enthusiasm, escalating his willingness to kill

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I should have had you killed.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: I can do that. I'll get to it right after I feed the birds.

jokes.text: Yeah, listen, I'm a little under the weather. I went on a booze cruise last week. It just finished up an hour ago.
jokes.setup: Joan admits she's under the weather
jokes.punchline: Reveals she's been on a week-long booze cruise that just ended
jokes.explanation: Good reveal about Joan's obvious intoxication with absurd timeline

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: I'm not feeling well.

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: I just got back from a week-long booze cruise.

jokes.text: On a completely unrelated note, I have horrible allergies.
jokes.setup: Joan just admitted to being drunk from a week-long booze cruise
jokes.punchline: She claims her obvious hangover symptoms are 'unrelated' allergies
jokes.explanation: Good ironic denial - obvious lie told with straight-faced confidence

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: It's not a hangover, it's allergies.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Joan, you just got back from a week-long booze cruise.

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: Yes, and I have allergies. Two unrelated things.

jokes.text: Today's show will not go on.
jokes.setup: Joan declares 'the show must go on' despite being drunk
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts herself by ending the show
jokes.explanation: Quick contradiction joke showing Joan's complete inability to function

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: The show must go on!

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: Actually, the show is over. Everyone go home.

jokes.text: Hey, Joan, settle a bet for me. Who's the sexiest couple in history? R-Patz and K-Stew, or Bieber--
jokes.setup: Caller asks Joan a serious relationship question
jokes.punchline: The 'serious' question is about teen celebrity couples using fan nicknames
jokes.explanation: Generic caller bit about celebrity culture, not particularly sharp

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: Joan, I have a serious relationship question.

dialogue.speaker: Joan
dialogue.line: Of course, what is it?

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: Do you think Zayn and Harry are still together, or is Zarry over?

jokes.text: We don't know where Jerry is.
jokes.setup: Ben updates Leslie on various problems with the gala
jokes.punchline: Jerry is still missing from the fake emergency hours later
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Jerry's continued panic, implies extended physical comedy

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, we have a problem. The caterer double-booked us, the band cancelled, and Jerry somehow got locked in the storage closet during setup.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry? Again? How is that even possible?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I don't know, but that's not even the worst part. We've been looking for him for hours. We declared a fake emergency to search the building.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: And Jerry is still missing.

jokes.text: I don't know how to answer that. / A simple "yes" or "no." / Well, everyone calls me Andy, but my full name is Andrew, I think, so... No? / Wait. Yes.
jokes.setup: Simple question about whether Andy's name is Andy
jokes.punchline: Andy overthinks the question and isn't even sure of his own name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - manages to be confused by the most basic question possible

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: Your name is Andy, correct?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, it depends on how you define 'is.' I mean, technically my birth certificate says Andrew, but everyone calls me Andy. So is Andy really my name, or is Andrew my name and Andy is just what people call me? And honestly, some days I don't even feel like an Andy. Some days I feel more like a... I don't know, a Derek?

jokes.text: Yes, I give it to him. When I was a kid, I always wanted to hold a cop's gun. To make that dream come true for another kid-- Well, that's what being a cop is all about.
jokes.setup: Scenario about a child wanting to hold a police weapon
jokes.punchline: Andy thinks giving children guns is the essence of police work
jokes.explanation: Excellent Andy logic - dangerously naive view of police work based on childhood dreams

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You don't want to let the kid hold the gun? That's like, the whole point of being a cop. That's police work right there.

jokes.text: Well, first, I would be like, "Dad... You're alive? What the hell? Also, do you know where my catcher's mitt is?"
jokes.setup: Scenario about pulling over your father for speeding
jokes.punchline: Andy reveals his father is dead and adds random question about baseball equipment
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment mixing dark revelation with Andy's childlike priorities

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, I can't pull over my dad for speeding.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My dad's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Also, what's the difference between a baseball glove and a baseball mitt?

jokes.text: She has a bad hangover, which she is pretending is allergies.
jokes.setup: Caller asks what's wrong with Joan
jokes.punchline: Ron states the obvious truth with characteristic bluntness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron honesty - cuts through Joan's obvious lies with brutal clarity

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: She's a woman.

jokes.text: Wouldn't know-- never been hungover. After I've had too much whiskey, I cook myself a large flank steak, pan-fried in salted butter. I eat that, put on a pair of wet socks, and go to sleep.
jokes.setup: Caller asks if Joan will be okay from her hangover
jokes.punchline: Ron describes his bizarre but apparently effective hangover prevention ritual
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ron moment - absurdly specific routine that somehow makes perfect sense for him

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: Will Joan be okay?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Oh, she'll be fine. I never get hangovers.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: The key is before you go to bed, you have to drink a glass of warm milk, do 50 push-ups, and think about Indians.

jokes.text: "I, Chris Traeger, after several sustained hours of diarrhea, combined with violent coughing and a devastating fever, followed by even more diarrhea, have succumbed to the avian flu." I'm dead.
jokes.setup: Chris reads his official death statement from the drill
jokes.punchline: His death involves escalating, graphic symptoms described with formal language
jokes.explanation: Good escalation comedy with Chris's formal announcement of his disgusting fictional death

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: In the event of my death, I want my remains to be cremated, and my ashes scattered in a place of natural beauty. However, if I am to die from a bizarre accident involving a drill, I accept that my final moments will be marked by a sudden onset of vertigo, followed by acute abdominal trauma, profuse hemorrhaging, loss of consciousness, and ultimately, catastrophic organ failure.

jokes.text: Woodworking, uh, novels about tall ships, meat, that sort of thing.
jokes.setup: Ron lists his interests for callers to ask about
jokes.punchline: The list perfectly encapsulates his manly, old-fashioned personality
jokes.explanation: Perfect character shorthand - three items that completely define Ron's interests

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: My interests are woodworking, fishing, hunting, carpentry, and the study of government.

jokes.text: Oh, really? Specifically asked that the drill to be done today and said I should give you everything I got.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizes something suspicious about the timing
jokes.punchline: Leonard reveals Jamm orchestrated the intensive drill timing
jokes.explanation: Plot revelation moment - confirms the sabotage suspicion

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... the timing of this intensive drill is awfully convenient.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Huggins
dialogue.line: Yeah, about that... Jamm orchestrated the whole thing.

jokes.text: It is with a heavy heart that I say, "We have been jammed."
jokes.setup: Leslie realizes Jamm has sabotaged them
jokes.punchline: She announces it with mock solemnity using 'jammed' as a verb
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie moment - turning Jamm's name into a verb with dramatic flair

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: We've been jammed.

jokes.text: In order to save our park... We have to destroy the entire town.
jokes.setup: Leslie needs a new strategy to escape the drill
jokes.punchline: Her solution is comically extreme and counterproductive
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - Leslie's desperation leads to absurd nuclear option

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I have a new strategy. We're going to dig ourselves out of this hole by digging an even bigger hole.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's what they said about the Internet, Ben. And look at us now.

jokes.text: Great. Why don't you tell the hospital director to flush them down the toilet.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks about remaining flu vaccine supplies
jokes.punchline: She orders them destroyed to worsen the fake crisis
jokes.explanation: Good follow-through on Leslie's plan to sabotage the drill

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me we still have flu vaccine supplies left?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Destroy them. All of them. We need this crisis to be as bad as possible.

jokes.text: Chief Fugleberg, I want you to order your officers to find all the infected birds in the area and perform CPR.
jokes.setup: Leslie continues giving counterproductive orders
jokes.punchline: Orders police to perform CPR on diseased birds
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist order - impossible task that makes no medical sense

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Orders to all units: we need you to perform CPR on any diseased birds you encounter in the park. This is a public health emergency.

jokes.text: Oh, no! Pawnee has been hit with... A tornado quake!
jokes.setup: Leslie decides to escalate the disaster scenario
jokes.punchline: Invents an impossible hybrid natural disaster
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation with made-up disaster portmanteau

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I've got it. What if we had a torricane?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: A what?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A tornado and a hurricane had a baby. It's a torricane. It has the unpredictability of a tornado with the destructive power of a hurricane.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's not a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Not yet it isn't.

jokes.text: Take a walnut and rub it into the legs of your table. That'll mask the scratches. Next thing you want to do is ditch the Terrier and get yourself a proper dog. Any dog under 50 pounds is a cat, and cats are pointless.
jokes.setup: Caller asks about repairing dog damage to furniture
jokes.punchline: Ron gives practical advice then escalates to eliminating small dogs entirely
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ron moment - practical help followed by philosophical stance on pets

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: How do I repair dog damage to my furniture?

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: There are several options. You can use wood filler for small scratches, or refinish the entire piece. Alternatively, you could get rid of the dog.

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: I love my dog though.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Then I suggest a more permanent solution: eliminate all small dogs.

jokes.text: Banks are Ponzi schemes run by morons.
jokes.setup: Ron continues his call-in show rant
jokes.punchline: His characteristically blunt assessment of financial institutions
jokes.explanation: Perfect economical Ron wisdom - complex financial critique in six words

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Banks? They're just fancy legalized theft operations run by people in suits who are too cowardly to actually rob you themselves. At least a common criminal has the decency to be honest about what he's doing.

jokes.text: Whatever happened to, "Hey, I have some apples. Would you like to buy them?" "Yes, thank you." That's as complicated as it should be to open a business in this country.
jokes.setup: Ron continues ranting about business complications
jokes.punchline: Reduces ideal capitalism to the simplest possible transaction
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ron philosophy - libertarian ideals expressed through fruit metaphor

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Look, I don't care about your business complications. Capitalism is simple: I give you money, you give me a thing. That's it. No committees, no forms, no women.

jokes.text: I've seen three movies in my life-- Bridge on the River Kwai, Patton, and Herbie Fully Loaded. My girlfriend's kids love it. It's pretty funny.
jokes.setup: Ron lists his limited movie experience with two war classics
jokes.punchline: Third movie is a children's comedy, which he genuinely enjoyed
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - tough guy Ron's secret soft spot for kid's movies

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've only seen two movies in my life. Saving Private Ryan and Apocalypse Now. Both of them are masterpieces.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: The third movie I saw was a children's comedy. And I genuinely enjoyed it.

jokes.text: Everyone in Pawnee is dead. Including Councilman Jamm. / What? No, no, you can't do that. It says right there, you're dead, so is everyone you care about. / Oh, well, joke's on you. I don't have anyone I care about.
jokes.setup: Leonard announces everyone in town is dead, Jamm protests
jokes.punchline: Jamm's defense is that he has no loved ones to lose
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy reveal about Jamm's complete lack of relationships

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Everyone in town is dead.

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: That's not true! I have no loved ones to lose!

jokes.text: I can't hear you. I'm a ghost. / Yeah, well, so am I, so you can hear me! Ghost jammed!
jokes.setup: Leslie pretends being dead makes her unable to hear Jamm
jokes.punchline: Jamm uses ghost logic to continue arguing and adds his catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist argument using ghost rules, plus Jamm callback to his verbal tic

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Well, if you're a ghost, then you can't hear the living world, right? So anything I say right now doesn't count as harassment!

dialogue.speaker: Jamm
dialogue.line: Treat yo' self!

jokes.text: We got all the way to Muncie before we realized that it was just a drill. / I mean, all I'm saying is you could've called. / No one had your cell number, Big "J."
jokes.setup: Jerry finally returns from his panic evacuation
jokes.punchline: He drove to another city before realizing it was fake, then complains about communication
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to Jerry's extended absence with typical Jerry misunderstanding

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I drove all the way to Shelbyville before I realized it was a false alarm!

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Jerry, you drove to another city?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, nobody told me it was fake! If you're going to have an emergency evacuation, maybe use the phone tree or send an email or something!

jokes.text: I had a classic stroke of Haverford genius. Who has the most to lose from a new Paunch Burger? Their competitors.
jokes.setup: Tom explains how he solved the food problem
jokes.punchline: His 'genius' is the obvious business logic of approaching competitors
jokes.explanation: Good Tom self-aggrandizement for basic business insight

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I solved the food problem. I went to all the competitors and I asked them if they wanted to sell me their food at a discount. They said yes. Boom. Genius.

jokes.text: I had a classic stroke of Haverford genius. Who has the most to lose from a new Paunch Burger? Their competitors.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining how he solved the catering problem
jokes.punchline: Self-aggrandizing while revealing actually smart business thinking
jokes.explanation: Tom's rare moment of actual business acumen wrapped in his typical self-promotion. The logic is sound which makes his bragging earned.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I solved the catering problem by negotiating with three different vendors, playing them against each other, and leveraging my relationships to get a 40% discount. Some people call it business savvy. I call it the Tom Haverford special.

jokes.text: I also helped a child perform a tracheotomy on his elderly uncle. It's been a very rewarding day.
jokes.setup: Ron mentions his other activities during the day
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions helping with emergency surgery as if it's normal
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ron deadpan - treats emergency surgery as casual good deed

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I helped perform emergency surgery this morning. Then I went to the dentist.

jokes.text: And thanks for dressing up, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Leslie thanks Jerry for formal attire at the gala
jokes.punchline: Implies Jerry is inappropriately dressed after his day-long panic adventure
jokes.explanation: Standard Jerry mockery about his disheveled appearance

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, I just want to say thank you for cleaning up and wearing formal attire tonight. You look very handsome.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, thank you Leslie. Though I have to admit, after the day I've had, I'm not entirely sure this tuxedo is appropriate anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What do you mean? You look perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I spent the last four hours running through the woods, fell into a creek, and got chased by what I'm pretty sure was a bear. So technically, this tuxedo is now a wetsuit.

jokes.text: He got 100% on his written test-- first guy in history to do it-- but he failed his personality examination.
jokes.setup: Chief explains Andy's test results
jokes.punchline: Andy aced the academic portion but failed the personality test completely
jokes.explanation: Good ironic result - Andy's intelligence vs. terrible judgment perfectly summarized

dialogue.speaker: Police Chief
dialogue.line: Andy aced the academic portion but failed the personality test completely.

jokes.text: Official police wrist lock. You can't hit me. Try to hit me. Or here, no, try-- it's this hand. Wrist lock. Boom, too much pain, you can't even hit me. Try to hit me. If I had my gun, you wouldn't try to hit me, though, is the thing.
jokes.setup: Andy demonstrates police techniques to cope with failure
jokes.punchline: His demonstration is confused, ineffective, and ends with concerning gun comment
jokes.explanation: Good Andy physical comedy showing why he failed the personality test

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, so when you fail, you gotta just shake it off. Like this.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You do a little spin, you take a deep breath, and then you just... move on to the next thing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's what we do in the Police Academy. Well, actually, we do a lot of things with guns.

jokes.text: Have you ever thought of being a sperm donor? / I'm impotent. / Yeah, I don't need to know that.
jokes.setup: Chris asks Leonard a personal question
jokes.punchline: Leonard's blunt response makes Chris immediately regret asking
jokes.explanation: Good awkward comedy with Chris's attempt at small talk backfiring

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Leonard, can I ask you something personal?

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Have you ever thought of being a sperm donor? I'm impotent. Yeah, I don't need to know that.
jokes.setup: Leonard asking Ron an inappropriate personal question
jokes.punchline: TMI response followed by Ron's uncomfortable retreat
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable oversharing comedy. Leonard's inappropriate question gets an even more inappropriate answer.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Ron, how often do you and Tammy have relations?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's... that's not something I'm going to discuss with you, Leonard.

jokes.text: Let's get married... Tonight.
jokes.setup: Ben and Leslie discuss their wedding timeline
jokes.punchline: Ben's sudden decision to get married immediately at the gala
jokes.explanation: Romantic plot development with comedic spontaneity, though not primarily a joke

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We need to set a date. When do you want to get married?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I don't know, maybe next spring? We could do something really nice, plan it out properly.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Next spring? Ben, I want to marry you now!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, we can't just get married at the gala tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Because... okay, you know what? Let's do it. Let's get married tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Really. I don't want to wait another day.

jokes.text: the earthquake registered an 8.5 on the Richter scale. That's correct. It was a large one.
jokes.setup: Fake news report about massive earthquake
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement calling 8.5 earthquake 'large'
jokes.explanation: Deadpan understatement in fake disaster reporting. Calling a catastrophic earthquake 'large' is perfect scientific understatement.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: We're getting reports of an 8.5 magnitude earthquake that has devastated the entire West Coast.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: Experts are calling it a large earthquake.

tmdb_overview: Leslie and Ben organize a black tie gala to raise money for their park, but their plans are derailed when a city-wide emergency response drill is called. Meanwhile, Andy prepares to take his Police Academy Exam and Ron fills in for Leslie on Pawnee Today.
insight: Absurdist escalation drives a mid-tier entry: 76 jokes in 27 minutes, no standout moments.
